questions.question: Explain the difference between GL_COMPILE and GL_COMPILE_AND_EXECUTE modes in display lists.
keyPoints: GL_COMPILE stores commands in the display list without executing them during definition
GL_COMPILE_AND_EXECUTE stores commands AND executes them immediately while creating the list
GL_COMPILE is used for normal display list creation
GL_COMPILE_AND_EXECUTE is useful for testing or one-time execution during compilation

questions.question: List three advantages and two disadvantages of using display lists in OpenGL.
keyPoints: Advantages: 1) Improved performance for repeated rendering 2) Reduced network traffic in client-server systems 3) Commands are pre-processed and optimized 4) Simplifies complex geometry rendering
Disadvantages: 1) Static data only - cannot modify after compilation 2) Memory overhead for storing lists 3) Not all OpenGL commands can be stored

questions.question: Describe the purpose of glPushMatrix() and glPopMatrix() in hierarchical modeling.
keyPoints: glPushMatrix() saves the current matrix state onto the matrix stack
glPopMatrix() restores the previously saved matrix state
They allow isolation of transformations for different objects
Essential for building hierarchical/model tree structures
Prevents transformations from affecting unrelated objects

questions.question: What is hierarchical modeling? How does it simplify complex scene creation?
keyPoints: Hierarchical modeling builds complex objects from simpler sub-components
Uses a tree structure with parent-child relationships
Transformations applied to parent automatically propagate to children
Reduces code duplication by reusing component models
Example: robot arm - moving shoulder moves entire arm including hand

questions.question: Explain the three basic modeling transformations in computer graphics.
keyPoints: Translation: Moves object along X, Y, Z axes using glTranslate()
Rotation: Rotates object around an axis using glRotate()
Scaling: Resizes object along each axis using glScale()
These are affine transformations that modify object position, orientation, or size
All transformations are represented as matrices and combined in the model-view matrix

questions.question: Why is the order of matrix operations important in OpenGL transformations?
keyPoints: Matrix operations in OpenGL are applied in reverse order (right to left)
To achieve translate→rotate→scale, specify in order: scale, rotate, translate
This is because matrices multiply on the left
Incorrect order produces different results
Example: rotate then translate vs translate then rotate give different final positions

questions.question: Write the OpenGL code sequence to create and execute a display list that draws a triangle.
keyPoints: glNewList(1, GL_COMPILE); // Begin list ID 1
glBegin(GL_TRIANGLES);
glEnd();
glEndList(); // End list definition
glCallList(1); // Execute the display list

questions.question: Explain the complete process of creating, managing, and using display lists in OpenGL. Include function syntax, parameters, and practical considerations.
keyPoints: glNewList(listID, mode) begins definition with unique ID and compile mode (GL_COMPILE or GL_COMPILE_AND_EXECUTE)
Place all OpenGL commands between glNewList and glEndList
glEndList() completes the definition and compiles commands
glCallList(listID) executes a single list
glCallLists() can execute multiple sequential lists
glDeleteLists() frees memory when lists are no longer needed
Cannot nest display lists or modify after compilation
Best for static geometry rendered repeatedly

questions.question: Explain hierarchical modeling with a detailed example of a robotic arm. Include how matrix operations and the matrix stack are used.
keyPoints: Hierarchical modeling structures complex objects as parent-child relationships
Robotic arm example: shoulder → upper arm → elbow → forearm → hand
Base (shoulder) is parent, upper arm is child
Apply glPushMatrix() before each joint transformation
glTranslate() positions each joint, glRotate() rotates at joint
glPopMatrix() returns to parent coordinate system
Moving shoulder (parent) moves entire arm (all children)
Moving hand (leaf) only moves hand itself
Matrix stack preserves parent transformations while computing child transformations
This allows articulated motion with minimal code

questions.question: Describe the three basic modeling transformations (translation, rotation, scaling) in detail with their OpenGL function syntax, parameters, and examples.
keyPoints: glTranslate{fd}(x, y, z): Moves object by adding (x,y,z) to coordinates. Example: glTranslatef(2.0, 0.0, 0.0) moves 2 units right
glRotate{fd}(angle, x, y, z): Rotates by angle (degrees) around axis (x,y,z). Example: glRotatef(90.0, 0.0, 0.0, 1.0) rotates 90° around Z-axis
glScale{fd}(x, y, z): Scales by factors along each axis. Example: glScalef(2.0, 0.5, 1.0) doubles width, halves height
All functions modify the current matrix
glLoadIdentity() resets before transformations
Matrix multiplication order determines final transformation
Negative scale values create reflections

questions.question: Compare display lists with vertex buffer objects (VBOs). When would you choose one over the other?
keyPoints: Display Lists: Pre-compiled commands, read-only after compilation, good for static geometry, simpler API
VBOs: Store vertex data in GPU memory, can be dynamically updated, more flexible
Choose Display Lists when: geometry is static, rendered repeatedly, simpler implementation needed
Choose VBOs when: geometry changes frequently, need partial updates, better performance for large datasets
Modern OpenGL favors VBOs over display lists
Display lists are deprecated in modern OpenGL versions (3.0+)
VBOs offer better performance and more control over data
